name: Cova des Diners
distance: 105 m from where the vehicle is parked
access: Own vehicle, or take the Porto Cristo bus from Palma
equipment: Most of the cave is accessible without vertical progression gear, though some shafts at the extremes require it. A 30 m rope, sling, and a couple of rope protectors are sufficient to access any part of the cave.
description: A labyrinthine cave classified as a Site of Community Importance (LIC) under EU protection, requiring official permission to visit. The cave features large formations, impressive gours, a notable fault mirror spanning 40 m, and the dramatic 'Descent to Hell' area. Navigation is extremely challenging; a marked route has been designed for first-time visitors to prevent getting lost. The cave has the highest conservation grade and must not be contaminated with candles or markers.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova des Diners is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI.

At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

Introduction

Due to the difficulty that exists in this labyrinthine cave for orientation, we have designed a route that can serve well for a first visit, said route covering a surface large enough to give a perspective of the cave, but we must not let our guard down since even with this layout one must take the utmost care not to get lost. Under no circumstances should candles or anything else that could contaminate the cave be brought inside to mark the way back, as doing so could lead to its contamination and ultimately a serious fine from the Ministry of the Environment. One must not forget that the Cova des Diners is under the European Union and has the highest level of conservation.

Route Signposting

On the topographic map that accompanies this cave, a route has been designed marked in green with lines of two thicknesses: the thicker one allows us to follow the route, while the thin one allows us to make side trips and return. On this route, the use of vertical progression equipment is not necessary, nor is any rope, although one must always be watchful not to slip or put one's boot into any of the many "traps" that exist throughout the cave. We have also designed circles with a black background and orange numbers that are placed strategically on the topo map to help us locate ourselves.

Description

From the entrance to point 1

We will access the cave through the door that is fitted to be closed with a metal barrier but which is currently dismantled inside (square with green background on the topographic map). Once inside, head toward the other entrance, clearly visible by the light entering from outside. A small escarpment of approximately 2 m must be descended to continue advancing through a gallery until it widens. As a reference, on the left side one can observe an elegant colonnade of more or less one meter in height (point 1 on the topo).

From point 1 to point 2

If we ascend along the flowstone, we will soon encounter steps carved into it, and a little further ahead a "wall" that we must pass through. Once on the other side of the "wall," we find ourselves at point 2 on the topo.

From point 2 to point 3

We begin the descent from point 2 between columns of considerable size, one of them affected by a subsidence process at its midpoint. The distance between point 2 and point 3 is around 20 m in length and ends right where the cave becomes more spacious and the floor practically horizontal.

From point 3 to point 4

Heading 200° N with a length of 20 m, in a gentle descent to point 4. Along the way we will encounter formations of great size and thickness, as well as some gours on our right, while at the height of point 4, on our left after a brief descent, we will reach a gour of considerable size.

From point 4 to point 5

This section heading 160° and approximately 18 m ends right at a large rock fallen from the ceiling that has left a gap in the ceiling shaped like a "teepee" (like a Canadian tent). This section is not of great interest.

From point 5 to point 6

We will begin by climbing to the highest area of the rock, and from it we will start walking 30 m heading 100° N, this time through a low-ceilinged zone where we can never stand upright until the end of it, where we must downclimb on our right over a small escarpment.

From point 6 to point 7

In this section of approximately 35 m heading 190° N, we will pass through a zone where the ceiling is even lower than in the previous section, and on our left an escarpment will accompany us for the entire stretch, giving way to the most complex and demanding zone of the cave. In the final part of the section.

From point 7 to point 8

40 m in length heading 325° N, this brings us to the vicinity of the Baixada a l'Infern (Descent to Hell), which we will reach after descending an escarpment where the ceiling is very low.

From point 8 to point 9

This 20 m section is the one that definitively places us at the head of the Baixada a l'Infern, and its heading is 250° N.

From point 9 to point 10

This route is for taking a look at the Baixada a l'Infern as far as we see fit, heading south, and then returning to the starting point and subsequently to point 8.

From point 8 to point 11

A round-trip route heading 280° N, through a gentle slope until we overlook the East Hall.

From point 8 to point 5

This 13 m section heading 40° N connects with point 5 and point 4, previously visited.

From point 4 to point 12

This section of approximately 35 m in length with a heading of 285° N approaches nearly the western side of the cave. Along the way we will pass between formations of great size in the final part. Of note is the impressive fault mirror that can be contemplated on the left, with a length of 40 m.

From point 12 to point 13

Approximately 20 m in length at 30° N, it ends right at a point where a crack in the floor begins, more than 20 m long, and will serve us for making an excursion to the Sala de sa Basseta in the next section. On our right, the possibly tallest cone of collapse blocks in the cave can be seen.

From point 13 to point 14

We will deviate from the route by a few meters to enter a beautiful chamber, which is none other than the Sala de sa Basseta, accessed heading 312° N over 10 m through a narrow passage where a small pit must also be carefully negotiated. The visit to this place is worthwhile, after which we return to the starting point.

From point 13 to point 15

First heading 45° N over 40 m in length, this relatively comfortable section crosses a block of large dimensions. It also hides some interesting zones to the west, though of difficult access.

From point 15 to point 3

Covering 37 m heading 170° N through perhaps the most comfortable zone of the cave, which is not lacking in formations, some of them of great size. Along the way we will encounter some gours that at certain times of the year are covered with water. We pass through point 3.

From point 3 to point 2

As we already know it, I will take the liberty of not providing any description.

From point 2 to point 1

On this occasion we will exit with an approximate heading of 345° N, although perhaps this data may not be too necessary, since we only need to keep in mind that we must advance "hugging" the right side, and that will be sufficient. As soon as we begin, we will encounter a first gour of some depth, and if it holds water, we may end up putting a foot in it. A little further on we will find another that usually stores a large quantity of calcite particles. We continue walking in a gentle descent, being careful not to miss a beautiful large gour on our right behind a small flowstone. Once past it, we continue advancing through low-ceilinged terrain, always keeping the reference that we must not stray too far from the wall that always runs on our right, until we reach a new gour that complicates our passage with water, but which can be navigated on its left side between two columns. The height of the cave becomes slightly more generous though without allowing us to stand upright. Never losing the wall on our right, we will head toward point 1, leaving on our left ground that has been disturbed for reasons that are not relevant to explain now. The column mentioned at point 1 has had a small cord tied around it for a long time. From this point, the exit should no longer cause us any problems.
